rules.title: EC2/Compute Instance Missing Subnet Parent
rules.description: Compute instances (EC2, GCP Compute Engine) should be children of subnets in the hierarchy. Missing parent_type indicates instances will render at root level instead of inside network boundaries.
rules.detection_logic: Check resources where resource_type IN (aws_instance, google_compute_instance, oci_core_instance, alicloud_instance) and parent_resource_id IS NULL
remediation: Ensure all instances have parent_resource_id pointing to their subnet/network resource
In Terraform, link instances to subnets via subnet_id or network_interface references
Verify resource_type_db.py has parent_type set correctly for instance types

rules.title: Subnet Missing VPC Parent
rules.description: Subnets should always be children of VPCs in the hierarchy. Missing parent relationship breaks network containment visualization.
rules.detection_logic: Check resources where resource_type IN (aws_subnet, google_compute_subnetwork, azurerm_subnet, oci_core_subnet) and parent_resource_id IS NULL
remediation: Ensure all subnets have parent_resource_id pointing to their VPC
In Terraform, link subnets to VPCs via vpc_id or network references
Verify resource_type_db.py has parent_type set for subnet types

rules.title: Serverless Functions Incorrectly Categorized
rules.description: Cloud Functions, Lambda, and other serverless compute should be categorized as 'Compute' tier, not 'Data'. Incorrect categorization causes functions to render in wrong architectural tier.
rules.detection_logic: Check resources where resource_type IN (google_cloudfunctions_function, aws_lambda_function, azurerm_function_app, oci_functions_function, alicloud_fc_function) and verify category field equals 'Compute'
remediation: Verify resource_type_db.py has category='Compute' for all serverless function types
Functions are compute resources (they execute code), not data storage
Run diagram generation after fix to verify tier placement

rules.title: Attachment Relationship Modeled as Containment
rules.description: Some relationships are attachment (e.g., Security Group to NIC) not containment. Modeling as parent_type breaks hierarchy semantics.
rules.detection_logic: Check if resources like Security Groups, NICs, or VPC attachments use parent_type instead of resource_connections
examples: aws_security_group should NOT be parent of instances; instead use resource_connections with attachment semantics
aws_network_interface should be child of subnet, but Security Groups attach via separate mechanism
VPC is NOT a container for instances; instances reference VPC via subnet membership
remediation: Use parent_type ONLY for true hierarchical containment (VPC→Subnet→Instance)
Use resource_connections for many-to-many attachments (Security Group→NIC, VPC→Lambda)
Document attachment semantics in connection_type field (vpc_attachment, sg_attachment)

rules.title: Compute Instances Not Scoped to Zones/Availability Zones
rules.description: Instances should be modeled as children of zones (GCP) or availability zones (AWS). Missing zone parent breaks geographic scoping in diagrams.
rules.detection_logic: Check resources where resource_type IN (aws_instance, google_compute_instance) and parent_resource_id points to subnet instead of zone
remediation: AWS: Instances could have parent_type=aws_availability_zone, but typically only show subnet parent
Consider if zone-level scoping is critical for your security analysis
If using subnets (which are zone-scoped), zone parent is often optional

rules.title: Network Interface Resource Hierarchy
rules.description: Network Interfaces should be children of subnets, not instances. Security Groups and other resources attach to NICs, not instances directly.
rules.detection_logic: Check resources where resource_type IN (aws_network_interface, azurerm_network_interface) and verify parent_type=subnet
remediation: Ensure NICs have parent_resource_id pointing to their subnet
Security Groups should attach to NICs via resource_connections, not be parents of instances
In diagrams, NICs can be hidden (display_on_architecture_chart: false) since they're typically transparent
